items.correct: Energy used to maintain basic bodily functions at complete rest.
items.why: Students think BMR is about exercise. It's the baseline energy needed just to STAY ALIVE - breathing, heart, brain, organ function - typically 60-70% of total daily energy use even before any activity.

items.q: What is the difference between a food allergy and an intolerance?
items.correct: An allergy involves the immune system; intolerance does not.
items.misconception: They are the same with different severity.
items.random_wrong: Allergies are temporary, intolerances are permanent.
items.why: Students treat these as severity points on one spectrum. They have different mechanisms - allergies trigger immune (IgE) responses (potentially fatal anaphylaxis); intolerances are digestive (e.g., lactase deficiency).

items.q: What is the purpose of a star profile?
items.correct: Comparing multiple sensory attributes of products visually on a radar diagram.
items.misconception: Awarding overall scores out of 5 stars.
items.why: Students confuse star profiles with rating systems. Star profiles plot attributes (sweetness, crunch, saltiness, etc.) on radial axes - the SHAPE shows the product's sensory fingerprint, not its quality rating.

items.q: RNI meets the needs of what % of UK population?
items.why: It's 97.5%. “50%” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: NRV. “LRNI” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If RNI of vitamin C is 40 mg/day and an adult takes 25 mg/day, the shortfall (in mg) is:
items.why: The real figure is 15. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
